stem: A 32-year-old woman with polycystic ovary syndrome presents 6 days after IVF oocyte retrieval with rapidly worsening abdominal distension, nausea and shortness of breath. She required collection of 28 oocytes and had a fresh embryo transfer 2 days ago. She is now unable to keep fluids down and has had no urine for 8 hours.

data_table.Examination: Tense ascites, dull lung bases bilaterally, no peripheral oedema; abdomen distended and tender
data_table.Investigations: Hb 168 g/L (was 122), haematocrit 0.52, WCC 16, Na 130 mmol/L, K 5.2 mmol/L, creatinine 142 micromol/L (was 60), albumin 28 g/L, beta-hCG 220 IU/L

lead_in: Which is the single most important next intervention?

options.text: Admit for intravenous crystalloid resuscitation with thromboprophylactic enoxaparin
options.rationale: Severe OHSS with intravascular depletion (Hct 0.52, oligoanuria, creatinine 142) demands inpatient IV fluid resuscitation and pharmacological thromboprophylaxis with LMWH due to high VTE risk. (Source: RANZCOG OHSS 2022; FSA OHSS guideline)
source_refs: Fertility Society of Australia OHSS guideline

options.text: Commence high-dose oral cabergoline 0.5 mg daily and discharge with daily review
options.rationale: Cabergoline is preventive (started at trigger) and adjunctive; in severe OHSS with intravascular depletion it does not replace IV resuscitation and admission. (Source: RANZCOG OHSS 2022)

options.text: Arrange urgent paracentesis as first measure to relieve abdominal pressure and dyspnoea
options.rationale: Paracentesis is reserved for tense ascites failing IV fluids or causing respiratory compromise after volume resuscitation, not the first step. (Source: RANZCOG OHSS 2022)

options.text: Prescribe oral diuretics (furosemide 40 mg daily) to mobilise the third-space fluid quickly
options.rationale: Diuretics in OHSS worsen intravascular depletion and renal injury and are contraindicated in initial management. (Source: RANZCOG OHSS 2022)

options.text: Continue luteal phase progesterone and review symptomatically in the fertility clinic next week
options.rationale: Severe OHSS is a medical emergency; outpatient review is inappropriate and risks deterioration or VTE. (Source: RANZCOG OHSS 2022)

explanation.summary: OHSS results from gonadotrophin- or hCG-driven vascular leak after assisted reproduction. Severity ranges from mild (bloating) to severe/critical (third-spacing, intravascular depletion, renal failure, pleural effusions, VTE, ARDS). The pathognomonic biochemistry is haemoconcentration with paradoxical hyponatraemia. AU first-line is admission, isotonic crystalloid (around 1000 mL initially then titrate), strict fluid balance, LMWH thromboprophylaxis and paracentesis only if tense ascites fails to respond. Cabergoline is preventive; diuretics are contraindicated.
key_points: Severe OHSS = haemoconcentration, oligoanuria, ascites, dyspnoea.
Treat with IV crystalloid resuscitation and LMWH thromboprophylaxis.
Paracentesis only if tense ascites unresponsive to fluids or respiratory compromise.
Diuretics worsen intravascular depletion and are avoided.
Pregnancy worsens course - elevated beta-hCG sustains the syndrome.
explanation.pearls: The single biggest risk factor for severe OHSS is PCOS combined with high antral follicle count - prevent it with GnRH agonist trigger and freeze-all strategy in any cycle with more than 18 follicles or oestradiol over 18,000 pmol/L on trigger day.

sources.label: Fertility Society of Australia OHSS guideline

stem: A 34-year-old woman is being followed up after a complete hydatidiform mole evacuated 10 weeks ago. Her serial weekly beta-hCG has been: 32,400 then 4,100 then 580 then 220 then 195 then 185 then 188 IU/L. She has no vaginal bleeding and her cervix is closed; chest is clear and there is no abdominal mass. She is using effective contraception and her FIGO score is 4.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74, HR 76, T 36.6°C; physical examination unremarkable
data_table.Examination: Abdominal exam: no masses; speculum: closed os, normal vault
data_table.Investigations: Pelvic ultrasound: small intrauterine echogenic focus only, no invasive disease; CXR clear; routine bloods normal

lead_in: Which management plan is most appropriate?

options.text: Refer to a gestational trophoblastic disease centre for single-agent methotrexate chemotherapy
options.rationale: FIGO criteria for GTN are met (plateau over 3 weeks); FIGO score 4 (low risk) is treated with single-agent methotrexate or actinomycin-D via a national GTD centre. (Source: RANZCOG GTD 2023; eviQ GTN)

options.text: Continue weekly beta-hCG monitoring for another 8 weeks before considering active treatment
options.rationale: Four consecutive plateaued values (within 10 percent) over 3 weeks meets FIGO 2021 criteria for gestational trophoblastic neoplasia and mandates treatment now, not further surveillance. (Source: RANZCOG GTD 2023)

options.text: Perform a repeat suction evacuation under anaesthetic this admission to clear residual trophoblast
options.rationale: Repeat evacuation does not improve outcomes and risks uterine perforation; chemotherapy is the AU first-line for GTN. (Source: RANZCOG GTD 2023)

options.text: Commence multi-agent EMA-CO chemotherapy as primary treatment for residual molar disease now
options.rationale: Multi-agent EMA-CO is reserved for high-risk GTN (FIGO score >= 7); this patient scores 4 and warrants single-agent therapy. (Source: eviQ GTN)

options.text: Recommend total abdominal hysterectomy as definitive curative treatment given completed family
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is reserved for placental site trophoblastic tumour, drug-resistant disease, or patient choice with completed family; first-line for low-risk GTN remains chemotherapy. (Source: RANZCOG GTD 2023)

explanation.summary: Post-molar surveillance uses weekly beta-hCG to detect gestational trophoblastic neoplasia (GTN) - which occurs in around 15 percent after complete moles and 1 percent after partial moles. FIGO 2021 criteria diagnose GTN if hCG plateaus (within 10 percent) for 4 values over 3 weeks, rises 10 percent or more for 3 values over 2 weeks, persists for 6 months post-evacuation, or metastatic disease is identified. Low-risk disease (FIGO score under 7) is treated with single-agent methotrexate (or actinomycin-D for methotrexate resistance); high-risk disease (>= 7) requires multi-agent EMA-CO. Treatment is centralised through national GTD registries.
key_points: Post-mole monitoring: weekly hCG until 3 consecutive normal, then monthly for 6 months.
Diagnose GTN on plateau, rise, persistence over 6 months, or metastases.
FIGO score under 7 = low risk, single-agent methotrexate.
FIGO score >= 7 = high risk, multi-agent EMA-CO.
Avoid pregnancy for at least 6 months after hCG normalisation.
explanation.pearls: Choriocarcinoma can present years after a normal pregnancy as haemoptysis, neurological signs, or amenorrhoea with a positive pregnancy test - check beta-hCG in any reproductive-age woman with unexplained metastatic disease.

stem: An 8-month-old previously well infant is brought to ED in winter with 3 days of coryza, cough and worsening work of breathing. He has been feeding only one-third of normal and his last wet nappy was 6 hours ago. He has had two doses of low-flow oxygen at 1 L/min but remains tachypnoeic with subcostal recession. His parents have not yet given him any nebulised medications.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168, RR 68, SpO2 92% on 1 L/min nasal cannulae, T 38.0°C
data_table.Examination: Moderate subcostal and intercostal recession, bilateral wheeze and fine crackles, alert, capillary refill 2 s
data_table.Investigations: Nasopharyngeal swab: RSV positive; CXR not performed; cap gas pH 7.34 pCO2 48 mmHg HCO3 25

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in respiratory support?

options.text: Increase nasal cannula flow to 3 L/min and continue current observations with oral feeds
options.rationale: Standard low-flow cannulae above 2 L/min in infants is not better tolerated and does not provide positive pressure; HFNC is the next escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg every 20 minutes for three doses and reassess
options.rationale: Salbutamol does not benefit infants with bronchiolitis (under 12 months) and is not recommended in AU CPGs. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Give nebulised hypertonic saline 3 percent over 15 minutes and reassess work of breathing
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline has not shown consistent benefit in bronchiolitis and is not part of AU first-line management. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence intravenous dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg daily for 3 days and cease nasal oxygen
options.rationale: Corticosteroids do not improve outcomes in bronchiolitis and stopping oxygen at SpO2 92 percent risks deterioration. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence high-flow nasal cannula oxygen at 2 L/kg/min with continuous monitoring on ward
options.rationale: PARIS trial criteria are met (SpO2 under 92 percent on standard oxygen or persistent work of breathing); AU practice escalates to HFNC at 2 L/kg/min with reassessment at 1 hour. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; PARIS trial)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis in infants under 12 months with viral coryzal prodrome progressing to wheeze, crackles and respiratory distress. AU first-line is supportive care with feeding support and minimal-handling oxygen for SpO2 under 92 percent. The PARIS trial established HFNC at 2 L/kg/min as an effective rescue once standard cannulae fail, halving CPAP/intubation rates. Salbutamol, steroids, antibiotics, hypertonic saline and chest physio are NOT recommended; they neither shorten admission nor reduce escalation.
key_points: Diagnose clinically; CXR rarely needed.
Oxygen for SpO2 under 92 percent; minimal handling.
HFNC 2 L/kg/min for failure of standard oxygen (PARIS).
Salbutamol, steroids, antibiotics, hypertonic saline NOT routine.
Watch for apnoea in babies under 6 weeks or ex-preterm.
explanation.pearls: Failure of HFNC at 1 hour (HR not falling, RR not falling, SpO2 not improving) is a strong predictor of CPAP escalation - assess all three trajectories at the 60 minute mark rather than relying on absolute values alone.

stem: A 3-year-old girl is brought to the GP with 6 months of infrequent hard stools, soiling episodes most days and abdominal pain. She passes stool once every 4 to 5 days with straining and screaming. She started day-care 8 months ago and reduced fluid intake notably around that time. Her parents have tried prune juice and increased fibre without sustained improvement.

data_table.Vital signs: Weight 14 kg (50th centile), height 95 cm (50th centile), well-appearing afebrile child
data_table.Examination: Soft non-distended abdomen with palpable faecal mass in left iliac fossa; perianal exam: no fissures or anal abnormalities; growth following centile
data_table.Investigations: None at this stage; no red flags (no failure to thrive, no delayed meconium, normal spine and lower limb examination)

lead_in: Which management plan is most appropriate now?

options.text: Commence oral macrogol 3350 (Movicol Junior) escalating disimpaction regimen over 7 days
options.rationale: AU first-line for paediatric faecal impaction is escalating macrogol 3350 disimpaction over up to 7 days followed by maintenance dosing; safe and well tolerated. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Continue dietary measures (increased fluid, fibre, pear/prune juice) and review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Diet alone does not clear established faecal impaction; pharmacological disimpaction is required first. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Administer phosphate enemas daily for 3 days then commence oral lactulose maintenance
options.rationale: Phosphate enemas in toddlers carry risks of hyperphosphataemia and rectal trauma; oral macrogol is preferred when feasible. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Refer for manual disimpaction under general anaesthetic and surgical opinion within a fortnight
options.rationale: Manual disimpaction under GA is reserved for failure of medical disimpaction or anatomical concerns; not first-line. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Order anorectal manometry and barium enema to exclude underlying Hirschsprung disease today
options.rationale: Hirschsprung disease usually presents with delayed meconium and failure to thrive; functional constipation does not warrant manometry without red flags. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

explanation.summary: Functional constipation accounts for over 90 percent of childhood constipation; behavioural and dietary triggers (toilet training, daycare, low fluid intake) and pain-avoidance withholding are typical. Diagnosis is clinical; investigations are reserved for red flags (delayed meconium, failure to thrive, abnormal lower limb or spine examination, gross perianal pathology). Treatment is structured: disimpaction (oral macrogol over 7 days), then maintenance (macrogol daily for at least 3 to 6 months), behavioural training (timed toileting) and family education.
key_points: Clinical diagnosis; investigations only for red flags.
Macrogol 3350 disimpaction over up to 7 days.
Maintenance macrogol for 3 to 6 months minimum.
Aim 1 soft stool daily with no soiling.
Red flags: delayed meconium, FTT, lumbosacral signs, anal anomaly.
explanation.pearls: Soiling (encopresis) is a sign of overflow incontinence around a faecal mass, NOT diarrhoea or behavioural defiance; ceasing maintenance laxatives too early is the commonest cause of relapse - taper over weeks rather than stopping abruptly.

stem: A 22-month-old boy is brought to ED at 2 am with a 6-hour history of barking cough and noisy breathing that started after a coryzal illness. He is alert and consolable in his mother's lap. His parents have given paracetamol 2 hours ago for fever. He has no previous admissions and no chronic respiratory conditions.

data_table.Examination: Audible stridor at rest, mild intercostal recession, no cyanosis; speaks in short sentences when settled; chest clear on auscultation
data_table.Investigations: Not indicated; clinical diagnosis; Westley croup score 4 (moderate)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg every 20 minutes for 3 doses with continuous SpO2 monitoring
options.rationale: Salbutamol does not treat the upper-airway oedema of croup; it is not indicated. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg with intramuscular ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg as cover
options.rationale: IV steroids offer no advantage over oral in mild to moderate croup and antibiotics treat bacterial tracheitis or epiglottitis, not viral croup. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 1:1000 5 mL with ICU bed booked for likely escalation overnight
options.rationale: Adrenaline is added for SEVERE croup (Westley >= 8 or marked distress); moderate croup with rest stridor responds first to oral dexamethasone. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Single oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg and observe for at least 2 hours in the department
options.rationale: Moderate croup with stridor at rest is treated with a single dose of oral dexamethasone 0.15 to 0.6 mg/kg plus observation; nebulised adrenaline is added for severe distress. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Reassure parents, recommend cool mist humidifier at home and discharge with safety netting
options.rationale: Stridor at rest indicates moderate croup needing steroid and observation; cool mist has no evidence of benefit. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

explanation.summary: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis) is a clinical diagnosis in children 6 months to 6 years with a viral prodrome, barking cough and inspiratory stridor (usually parainfluenza). Westley score stratifies mild (0 to 2), moderate (3 to 5), severe (6 to 11) and impending respiratory failure (>= 12). Oral dexamethasone benefits all severities. Nebulised adrenaline (epinephrine) provides bridging vasoconstriction in severe disease; rebound at 2 hours mandates observation. Antibiotics, salbutamol and humidifiers do not improve outcomes.
key_points: Westley score 3 to 5 = moderate (stridor at rest, mild recession).
Dexamethasone 0.15 to 0.6 mg/kg PO single dose.
Adrenaline 1:1000 5 mL nebulised for severe (Westley >= 6).
Observe at least 4 hours after adrenaline due to rebound.
Antibiotics, salbutamol, humidifiers NOT indicated.
explanation.pearls: Sudden deterioration in an apparently moderate croup with high fever, drooling and inability to lie down should prompt suspicion of bacterial tracheitis or epiglottitis - avoid attempts at IV access or oral examination until anaesthetic-led airway support is at hand.
